返回本批次

尼泊尔的假救援球拍内幕

Inside Nepal's Fake Rescue Racket

kathmandupost.com 作者 lode 分数 255 评论 114 2026/04/02 19:40
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 在尼泊尔，无论以何种标准衡量，高空直升机救援都是真正的救生行动。在高海拔地区，氧气稀薄，天气变化无预警，能够在数小时内将受伤的徒步旅行者空运至加德满都，挽救了无数生命。但通过这一合法系统，利用其紧迫性、不透明性和远离监督的优势，形成了世界上最复杂的保险欺诈网络之一。尼泊尔的虚假救援骗局并不新鲜。 《加德满都邮报》于 2018 年首次曝光此事。几个月后，政府召集了一个实况调查委员会，制作了一份 700 页的报告，并宣布了改革。 2019年2月，《加德满都邮报》发表了一份长篇调查报告。去年，尼泊尔警方中央调查局重新打开了档案，他们发现欺诈行为并没有停止，反而愈演愈烈。这个骗局是如何运作的？假救援骗局的机制很简单：制造医疗紧急情况，呼叫直升机，将游客送往医院，然后提交一份与实际情况几乎没有相似之处的保险索赔。但复杂性在于如何对链条中的每个环节进行补偿，以及对于一家在澳大利亚和英国运营的外国保险公司来说，核实在偏远的喜马拉雅山谷 3,000 米处发生的事件有多么困难。CIB 的调查确定了制造“紧急情况”的两种主要方法。第一种方法涉及那些根本不想走回去的游客。在完成艰巨的徒步旅行后（例如珠穆朗玛峰大本营徒步旅行可能需要长达两周的步行时间），导游提供了另一种选择：假装生病，然后直升机就会来。剩下的就由指南来处理。第二种方法比较麻烦。在海拔3000米以上的地方，轻微的高原反应症状很常见。血氧饱和度会下降，手脚刺痛，头痛。在大多数情况下，只需休息、补充水分或逐渐下降即可。但根据 CIB 的调查，导游和酒店工作人员接受了在此时此刻恐吓徒步旅行者的培训。他们告诉他们，他们正面临死亡的危险，只有立即撤离才能拯救他们。在某些情况下，调查人员发现，用于预防高原反应的 Diamox（乙酰唑胺）片剂与过量饮水一起服用，会引发需要救援的症状。在调查中至少引用的一个案例中，发酵粉被混入食物中，导致游客身体不适。一旦“救援”被呼叫，财务编排就开始了。一架直升机可搭载多名乘客。但单独的全价发票会提交给每位乘客的保险公司，就好像每个乘客都有自己的专用航班一样。 4,000 美元的包机费用变为 12,000 美元的索赔。伪造航班舱单和装载单。在医院，医务人员使用从未参与该病例的高级医生的数字签名来准备出院摘要。在某些情况下，这些都是在医生不知情的情况下完成的。伪造的入院记录是为游客创建的，在一些有记录的情况下，这些游客在接受治疗时在医院食堂喝了啤酒。在一个案例中，Shreedhi 医院的一名办公室助理承认，他提供了自己大约一年前在另一家医院拍摄的 X 光报告，作为外国徒步旅行者治疗索赔保险的案例。警方审讯期间详细描述了维持该网络的委员会结构。医院向徒步旅行公司支付 20% 至 25% 的保险费，并向直升机救援运营商支付 20% 至 25% 的保险费，以换取患者转诊。徒步导游及其公司从虚高的发票中受益。在某些情况下，游客本身会获得现金奖励来参与。欺诈的实际规模有多大？CIB 调查得出的数字令人震惊。2022 年至 2025 年间，调查人员发现了 4,782 名外国患者在涉案医院接受治疗。其中，171例被确认为假救援。在此期间，Era 国际医院收到了与这些活动相关的超过 1587 万美元的押金。 Shreedhi 国际医院收到超过 122 万美元。 在救援运营商中，山地救援服务公司 (Mountain Rescue Service) 在 1,248 次包机航班中实施了 171 次欺诈性救援，向保险公司索赔约 1,031 万美元。尼泊尔包机服务公司在 471 架航班中实施了 75 次虚假救援，索赔 820 万美元。珠穆朗玛峰体验与援助与 601 架航班的 71 起可疑救援事件有关，保险索赔总额为 1,104 万美元。 警方记录了一起案件，在同一天，使用同一架直升机在同一架直升机上营救了 4 名游客，保险索赔总额为 1,104 万美元。直升机和清单。尽管如此，保险索赔还是以多次单独的救援形式提交，救援费用总额达到 31,100 美元，加上单独的医院费用 11,890 美元。 Shreedhi 医院的 Girwan Raj Timilsina 医生在审讯期间表示，仅在一个案例中，他的医院就向尼泊尔包机服务公司支付了约 910 万卢比的佣金，向 Heli on Call 支付了 150 万卢比，并向徒步运营商支付了 150 万卢比。他在一份录音声明中说：“我的医院还从收入中向徒步公司和救援公司提供佣金，以促进业务。”所有徒步者都被骗了吗？并非所有来尼泊尔徒步的外国人都是骗局受害者。根据 CIB 调查的证据，其中一些人愿意参与。 调查期间发现的 WhatsApp 交流显示，德国徒步旅行者 Petra Homens 向尼泊尔特许服务公司主席、该网络的关键人物之一拉宾德拉·阿迪卡里 (Rabindra Adhikari) 抱怨说，她似乎被双重计费。 “你们公司加倍收费！！！”她写道，并指出她的保险公司已经直接支付了直升机费用。 Adhikari 承认可能存在双重收费并提供退款。此次交流意义重大，因为它证实了出于保险目的而故意夸大直升机费用，并且同一个人也涉嫌虚假治疗索赔。 另一方面，两名加拿大徒步旅行者（邮报为了保护隐私而隐去了其姓名）于 2025 年底主动向 CIB 提出投诉，指控他们在 11 月的徒步旅行中存在欺诈性医疗后送。他们的投诉描述了一种现在熟悉的模式：向保险公司报告的氧气读数低得危险（50％至51％），不必要的CT扫描和入住ICU，以及医院夸大病情以证明文书工作的合理性。难道这个系统不应该得到修复吗？新的调查是关于尼泊尔机构失败的故事。 2018 年，继《加德满都邮报》早期报道后，政府实况调查委员会花了数月时间调查了 10 家直升机公司、6 家医院以及 36 家旅行和徒步机构。最终提交给当时的旅游部长拉宾德拉·阿迪卡里 (Rabindra Adhikari) 的 700 页报告记录了广泛存在的欺诈行为。它列举了针对单次直升机乘坐的多起保险索赔、迫使徒步旅行者同意不必要的空运的压力，以及食品掺假导致游客生病的指控。委员会建议所有直升机公司、医院、旅行社和保险公司向游客搜救委员会、旅游警察和旅游部提交救援航班和医疗的详细信息。中介机构将被取消，旅行社将在整个旅行过程中对其客户承担法律责任。但这一切都不起作用。CIB 负责人 Manoj Kumar KC 在最近接受《华盛顿邮报》采访时表示：“由于惩罚措施不严，骗局仍在继续。” “如果不采取行动打击犯罪，犯罪就会猖獗。结果，保险诈骗太猖獗了。”目前的调查于 2025 年 9 月 26 日触发，当时一个名为 Deshbhakta Gen Z 的公民团体向 CIB 提出了新的投诉，促使该局重新打开多年来搁置的档案。为什么保险索赔如此难以核实？大多数旅行保险保单都要求在疏散之前联系保险公司。在喜马拉雅山，在通信困难且许多地区没有适当手机信号的海拔高度，这种情况几乎不会发生。当保险公司接到通知时，疏散工作已经完成，患者已经在数百英里外的加德满都的一家医院里，当地的救援公司或徒步旅行运营商已经开始准备文件。大型保险公司拥有自己的 24/7 应急响应团队，而小型保险公司则将其外包给全球援助公司。当病例从尼泊尔到达时，这些公司通常会联系尼泊尔当地的援助公司，代表他们审查发票、医疗报告和航班清单。这是最容易隐藏欺诈行为的地方。当地援助公司与医院和救援机构在同一商业生态系统内运营。这家总部位于海外的保险公司正在依靠当地业务合作伙伴来验证这些中介机构可能帮助制作的文件。救援公司的动机是最大限度地增加每个航班的乘客数量，同时分别向每个保险公司收取费用。徒步公司的动机是将案件转介给支付最高佣金的救援人员。医院的动机是接纳病人、执行不必要的程序，并与为他们提供业务的经营者保持转介关系。这条链中的任何一点都不存在利益与支付索赔的保险公司一致的一方。本月早些时候提出的指控可能会发出强烈的信号。 2026 年 3 月 12 日，CIB 对 32 名个人提出危害国家罪和有组织犯罪指控。目前已逮捕九人，其余人据说正在潜逃。被指控的人包括来自三个直升机公司的操作员和员工：Mountain Helicopters、Manang Air（后更名为 Basecamp Helicopters）和 Altitude Air。它还对斯瓦肯国际医院、什里德国际医院和艾拉国际医院的医生和管理人员提出指控。案件记录包括闭路电视录像，证实了被指控病情危重的外国游客在其中一名被指控的医生经营的咖啡馆里喝啤酒，当时他们的医疗记录显示他们正在医院接受治疗。这对尼泊尔的徒步旅行行业意味着什么？对于将游客送往尼泊尔的国际保险公司、旅行社和徒步旅行机构来说，调查及其调查结果提出了令人不安的问题。 CIB 记录的欺诈行为并非少数流氓行为者所为，而是一个结构化的、以佣金为基础的网络，公开运营多年，涉及为患者提供服务的持证医疗专业人员，以及通过正规银行渠道处理数亿卢比的注册公司。2018 年骗局被发现后宣布的改革初衷是好的，但从未得到执行。目前的起诉是否会产生不同的结果，在很大程度上取决于尼泊尔法院是否施加足够严厉的处罚以改变商业计算。它还取决于旅游部是否建立了验证基础设施，以在支付之前捕获夸大的索赔。随着新政府本周宣誓就职，所有人都将关注如何处理此类事件，以及调查及其调查结果是否会得出有意义的结论。

Original Summary

  1. In Nepal, helicopter rescue on high altitude is, by any measure, a genuine lifesaving operation. At high altitude, where oxygen thins and weather changes without warning, the ability to airlift a stricken trekker to Kathmandu within hours has saved countless lives. But threaded through that legitimate system, exploiting its urgency, its opacity, and its distance from oversight, — is one of the most sophisticated insurance fraud networks in the world.Nepal’s fake rescue scam is not new. The Kathmandu Post first exposed it in 2018. Months later, the government convened a fact-finding committee, produced a 700-page report, and announced reforms. In February 2019, The Kathmandu Post published a long investigative report.Last year, Nepal Police’s Central Investigation Bureau reopened the file, and what they found is that the fraud did not stop — instead it was growing.How does the scam work?The mechanics of the fake rescue racket are straightforward: stage a medical emergency, call in a helicopter, check a tourist into a hospital, and file an insurance claim that bears little resemblance to what actually happened. But the sophistication lies in how each link in the chain is compensated, and how difficult it is for a foreign insurer — operating from Australia and the United Kingdom— to verify events that occurred at 3,000 metres in a remote Himalayan valley.The CIB investigation identifies two primary methods for manufacturing an “emergency.”The first involves tourists who simply don’t want to walk back. After completing a demanding trek — an Everest Base Camp trek, for instance, can take up to two weeks on foot — guides offer an alternative: pretend to be sick, and a helicopter will come. The guide handles the rest.The second method is more troubling. At altitudes above 3,000 metres, mild symptoms of altitude sickness are common. Blood oxygen saturation can drop, hands and feet tingle, headaches develop. In most cases, rest, hydration or a gradual descent is all that is needed. But guides and hotel staff, according to the CIB investigation, have been trained to terrify trekkers at precisely this moment. They tell them they are at risk of dying, that only immediate evacuation will save them. In some cases, investigators found that Diamox (Acetazolamide) tablets, used to prevent altitude sickness, were administered alongside excessive water intake to induce the very symptoms that would justify a rescue call.In at least one case cited in the investigation, baking powder was mixed into food to make tourists physically unwell.Once a “rescue” is called, the financial choreography begins. A single helicopter carries multiple passengers. But separate, full-price invoices are submitted to each passenger’s insurance company, as if each had their own dedicated flight. A $4,000 charter becomes a $12,000 claim. Fake flight manifests and load sheets are fabricated. At the hospital, medical officers prepare discharge summaries using the digital signatures of senior doctors who were never involved in the case. In some cases, these are done without those doctors’ knowledge. Fake admission records are created for tourists who were, in some documented instances, drinking beer in the hospital cafeteria at the time they were supposedly receiving treatment. Post file photoIn one case, an office assistant at Shreedhi Hospital admitted that he had provided his own X-ray report taken about a year ago at a different hospital, to be used as a case for treatment of foreign trekkers to claim insurance.The commission structure that holds the network together was described in detail during police interrogations. Hospitals pay 20 to 25 percent of the insurance payment to trekking companies and a further 20 to 25 percent to helicopter rescue operators in exchange for patient referrals. Trekking guides and their companies benefit from inflated invoices. In some cases, tourists themselves are offered cash incentives to participate.What is the actual scale of the fraud?The numbers that emerge from the CIB investigation are striking.Between 2022 and 2025, investigators identified 4,782 foreign patients treated across the implicated hospitals. Of these, 171 cases were confirmed as fake rescues. Over that period, Era International Hospital received deposits of more than $15.87 million linked to these activities. Shreedhi International Hospital received over $1.22 million.Among rescue operators, Mountain Rescue Service conducted 171 fraudulent rescues out of 1,248 total charter flights, claiming approximately $10.31 million from insurers. Nepal Charter Service carried out 75 fake rescues from 471 flights, claiming $8.2 million. Everest Experience and Assistance was linked to 71 suspicious rescues from 601 flights, with insurance claims totalling $11.04 million.In one instance that illustrates the brazenness of the scheme, police documented a case in which four tourists were rescued on a single helicopter flight, on the same date, using the same helicopter and manifest. Insurance claims were nonetheless submitted as multiple separate rescues, with the total rescue bill reaching $31,100, plus a separate hospital bill of $11,890.Dr Girwan Raj Timilsina of Shreedhi Hospital, speaking during interrogation, said that in one case alone, his hospital paid approximately Rs9.1 million in commissions to Nepal Charter Service, Rs1.5 million to Heli on Call, and a further Rs1.5 million to trekking operators. “My hospital has also given commission from its earnings to trekking companies and rescue companies to promote business,” he said in a recorded statement.Are all the trekkers scammed?Not all the foreign nationals who come to Nepal for trekking are scam victims. Some of them are willing participants, according to evidence in the CIB investigations.A WhatsApp exchange recovered during the investigation reveals a German trekker, Petra Homens, complaining to Rabindra Adhikari, the chairman of Nepal Chartered Service and one of the key figures in the network, that she appeared to have been double-billed. “Your company charged double!!!” she wrote, noting that her insurer had already paid the helicopter cost directly. Adhikari acknowledged that there may have been a double charge and offered a refund. Post file photoThe exchange is significant because it confirms that the helicopter bill had been deliberately inflated for insurance purposes, and that the same individual was also implicated in fake treatment claims.On the other side, two Canadian trekkers (whose names the Post is withholding to protect their privacy) proactively filed a complaint with the CIB in late 2025, alleging fraudulent medical evacuation during their November trek. Their complaint described a now-familiar pattern: oxygen readings reported to insurers as dangerously low (50 to 51 percent), unnecessary CT scans and ICU admissions, and hospitals that exaggerated conditions to justify the paperwork.Wasn’t the system supposed to be fixed?The new investigation is a story about institutional failure in Nepal. In 2018, following early reporting in The Kathmandu Post, a government fact-finding committee spent months investigating ten helicopter companies, six hospitals, and 36 travel and trekking agencies. The resulting 700-page report, which was submitted to then Tourism Minister Rabindra Adhikari, documented widespread fraud. It cited multiple insurance claims for single helicopter rides, pressure placed on trekkers to agree to unnecessary airlifts, and allegations that food was adulterated to make tourists sick.The committee recommended that all helicopter companies, hospitals, tour operators and insurance firms be required to submit details of rescue flights and medical treatment to the Tourist Search & Rescue Committee, the Tourist Police, and the Department of Tourism. Intermediaries were to be eliminated and tour operators were held legally responsible for their clients throughout a trip. Shutterstock But none of that worked.“The scam continued due to lax punitive action,” said Manoj Kumar KC, chief of the CIB, during a recent interview with the Post. “When there is no action against crime, it flourishes. The insurance scam too flourished as a result.”The current investigation was triggered on September 26, 2025, when a citizen group called Deshbhakta Gen Z filed a fresh complaint with the CIB, prompting the bureau to reopen files that had gone cold for several years.Why are insurance claims so hard to verify?Most travel insurance policies require the insurer to be contacted before an evacuation takes place. In the Himalayas, in altitudes where communication is a challenge and many areas are without proper cellphone signals, this almost never happens. By the time an insurer is notified, the evacuation is already complete, the patient is in a hospital hundreds of miles away in Kathmandu, and a local rescue company or trekking operator has already begun preparing the paperwork.Large insurers maintain their own 24/7 emergency response teams, while smaller ones contract this to global assistance companies. When a case arrives from Nepal, these companies typically contact a local Nepali assistance company to review invoices, medical reports, and flight manifests on their behalf.This is where the fraud is most easily hidden. The local assistance company operates within the same commercial ecosystem as the hospitals and rescue operators. The insurer, based overseas, is relying on local business partners to validate documents that those same intermediaries may have helped produce.The rescue company’s incentive is to maximise the number of passengers on each flight while billing each insurer separately. The trekking company’s incentive is to refer cases to rescue operators who pay the highest commissions. The hospital’s incentive is to admit patients, perform unnecessary procedures, and maintain referral relationships with the operators who send them business. At no point in this chain is there a party whose interests are aligned with the insurer paying the claim.The charges that were filed earlier this month could send a strong signal. On March 12, 2026, the CIB charged 32 individuals with offences against the state and organised crime. It has arrested nine people and the remaining are said to be absconding. Among those charged are operators and staff from three helicopter companies: Mountain Helicopters, Manang Air (since rebranded as Basecamp Helicopters), and Altitude Air. It has also charged physicians and administrators from Swacon International Hospital, Shreedhi International Hospital and Era International Hospital.The case records include CCTV footage confirming that foreign tourists reported as critically ill were filmed drinking beer at a cafe run by one of the charged physicians, at the time their medical records show them receiving hospital treatment.What does this mean for Nepal’s trekking industry?For international insurers, tour operators and trekking agencies that send tourists to Nepal, the investigation and its findings raise uncomfortable questions. The fraud documented by the CIB was not the work of a few rogue actors – it was a structured, commission-based network that operated openly for years, involved licensed medical professionals who vouched to serve patients, and registered companies that processed hundreds of millions of rupees through formal banking channels.The reforms that were announced after the scam was discovered in 2018 were well intentioned, but they were never enforced. Whether the current prosecutions produce a different outcome will depend in large part on whether Nepal’s courts impose penalties severe enough to alter the commercial calculus. It also depends on whether the Department of Tourism builds the verification infrastructure to catch inflated claims before they are paid. With a new government sworn in this week, all eyes will be on how incidents like this will be treated and whether investigations and their findings will reach a meaningful conclusion.

评论（默认中文）

sonink 2026/04/02 23:20
> 第二种方法比较麻烦。在海拔3000米以上的地方，轻微的高原反应症状很常见。血氧饱和度会下降，手脚刺痛，头痛。在大多数情况下，只需休息、补充水分或逐渐下降即可。 ...调查人员发现，用于预防高原反应的 Diamox（乙酰唑胺）片剂与过量饮水一起服用会诱发需要救援的症状。

这听起来不太准确。我已经徒步喜马拉雅山十多年了——AMS 的风险是非常真实的。与我一起徒步旅行的两个人在各自的喜马拉雅徒步旅行中因 AMS 死亡——两人之前都曾多次徒步旅行，并且非常清楚其中的风险。两次死亡事故的高度都在 12000-14000 英尺左右，远低于珠穆朗玛峰大本营的徒步高度。当 AMS 袭击时，您需要尽可能快地下降，并使用您可以使用的任何手段。否则，您已在不知不觉中进入了俄罗斯轮盘赌。

Diamox 被用作 AMS 的预防措施——同时摄入过量的水——这是所有高海拔喜马拉雅徒步旅行的标准指南。

> The second method is more troubling. At altitudes above 3,000 metres, mild symptoms of altitude sickness are common. Blood oxygen saturation can drop, hands and feet tingle, headaches develop. In most cases, rest, hydration or a gradual descent is all that is needed. ...investigators found that Diamox (Acetazolamide) tablets, used to prevent altitude sickness, were administered alongside excessive water intake to induce the very symptoms that would justify a rescue call.

This doesnt sound accurate. I have trekked the Himalayas for over a decade - the risks of AMS are very real. Two people I have trekked with have died due to AMS on separate himalayan treks - both had trekked multiple times before, and were well aware of the risks. Both the fatalities were around 12000-14000 feet - much below the Everest Base Camp trek. When AMS hits, you need to descend - as fast as possible, with whatever means you have at your disposal. Otherwise you have unknowingly entered a Russian Roulette.

And Diamox is used as a preventative course for AMS - alongside excessive water intake - this is standard guidelines in all high altitude himalayan treks.

在 HN 中查看
t0mas88 2026/04/03 05:34
12-14k 英尺附近的死亡事件还涉及其他问题吗？冷冻等？

根据航空规则，乘客可以无限期地在 12k 英尺高空无氧气条件下飞行。如果您在 10 千米至 13 千米之间持续超过 30 分钟，工作人员需要使用氧气。超过 13k 时，机组人员和乘客都必须立即使用氧气（EASA 规定，FAA 不同）。

所以他们似乎认为 12k 对乘客不会造成危险。

Were there other issues involved in the fatalities around 12-14k ft? Freezing etc?

In aviation rules you can have passengers at 12k ft without oxygen for an unlimited amount of time. The crew needs to use oxygen if you're between 10k and 13k for more than 30 minutes. Above 13k both crew and passengers must use oxygen immediately (EASA rules, FAA is different).

So they seem to consider 12k to not be dangerous to passengers.

在 HN 中查看
simplyluke 2026/04/03 06:58
根据山地经验：休息 12 千米和随后几天锻炼 12 千米会产生截然不同的反应。坐在飞机后座可能会让人感到轻微头痛，但可能是一个非常独特的 AMS 箱子，拖着一个包上下山。
Speaking from experience in the mountains: 12k at rest and 12k under subsequent days of exercise produce very different responses. What might be a mild headache sitting in the back of a plane could be a pretty distinct AMS case lugging a pack up and down mountains.

streetfighter64 2026/04/03 06:54
“你”饭后不会服用抗酸剂，除非“你”有胃酸问题或其他问题。中和胃酸通常不是明智之举，因为胃酸呈酸性是有原因的（消化食物）。有些人甚至建议吃饭时不要喝太多水，因为它会稀释胃酸，尽管这可能过于谨慎。我不知道在食物中添加小苏打是否会让你感觉更饱，但它肯定不会让你“感觉更好”，除非你有某种健康状况。
"You" don't take antacid after eating unless "you" have problems with your stomach acid or something. Neutralizing your stomach acid isn't generally a smart thing to do, because it's acidic for a reason (digesting food). Some people even recommend not drinking to much water with your meals because it dilutes your stomach acid, though that might be overly cautious. Whether adding baking soda to food makes you feel fuller I wouldn't know, but it certainly won't make you "feel better", unless you have some medical condition.

在 HN 中查看
EA-3167 2026/04/03 03:26
食物中可以检测到足以使人生病的苏打水量。作为自己制作蜂窝太妃糖和苏打面包的人来说，很容易搞乱比例并最终导致味道难闻的过量，而且过量的量相当小。

少量不会有什么不同，它只会刺激胃质子泵产生更多的 H+。

编辑：我读到的文章声称该骗局涉及烘焙 粉末，考虑到它更明显、更苦、更金属，这就更没有意义了。

An amount of soda sufficient to make you ill would be very VERY detectable in food. Speaking as someone who makes their own honeycomb toffee and soda bread, it's really easy to mess up the ratios and end up with an excess that tastes nasty, and that excess is pretty small.

A small amount won't make a different, it'll just stimulate a bit more H+ production from your stomach's proton pumps.

Edit: The article I read claims the scam involved baking powder, which makes even less sense given that it's even more noticeable, bitter and metallic.

ghaff 2026/04/02 23:33
Namche（该死的自动更正）集市是珠穆朗玛峰地区每个人都会经过的集市，高度超过 11000 英尺。 12-15K 英尺在计划中并不算高。美国西部的许多山峰都在这个范围内或更高。是的，适应环境时出现轻微头痛是很正常的。但还有什么，你需要下去。
Namche (damn autocorrect) Bazaar which everyone in the Everest region passes through is a bit over 11K feet. 12-15K feet just isn’t that high in the scheme of things. Many peaks in the western US are in that range or more. Yes, minor headaches are pretty normal when acclimatizing. But anything more, you need to go down.

gilesvangruisen 2026/04/03 04:47
我去过尼泊尔几次，到达我们位于 Namche 的旅馆后大约 1-2 小时就开始感觉到 AMS 症状。在接下来的几天里，Diamox 让我在前往 Tengboche 的途中保持良好状态，除了返回卢卡拉的直升机飞行外，我们最大行程约为 13,000 英尺。即使在美国海拔 9 千英尺的滑雪胜地也有类似的症状。它“不是那么高”，但足以诱发 AMS。
I've been to Nepal a few times and started feeling AMS symptoms about 1-2 hours after arriving at our lodge in Namche. Diamox kept me going great for the nxt few days on our way to Tengboche where we maxed out about 13k feet, besides the heli flight back to Lukla. Similar symptoms even at a ski resort at 9k feet in the US. It's "not that high" but plenty high to induce AMS.

在 HN 中查看
tristor 2026/04/02 23:48
我认为你指的是美国大陆，因为阿拉斯加和夏威夷被排除在外，而阿拉斯加与美国毗邻，但需要穿越加拿大的部分地区才能通过陆路到达。 也就是说，是的，惠特尼山是美国大陆最高的山峰，阿拉斯加的麦金利山是美国（以及美国本土）最高的山峰，也是世界上从山脚到山峰最高的山峰，也是世界上第三高的山峰。
I think you mean continental United States, as Alaska and Hawaii are excluded, where-as Alaska is contiguous with the United States, but requires crossing through parts of Canada to reach by land. That said, yes Whitney is the highest in the continental US, and McKinley in Alaska is the highest in the US (and contiguous US) and is also the tallest in the world from base to peak and the third most prominent peak in the world.
在 HN 中查看
shadow28 2026/04/02 23:52
实际上恰恰相反，美国大陆将包括阿拉斯加，因为它仍在北美大陆，而美国本土则不包括夏威夷和阿拉斯加。美国本土指的是美国本土 48 个州。
It's exactly the other way around actually, continental US would include Alaska since it's still on the North American continent whereas contiguous US excludes both Hawaii and Alaska. Contiguous US refers to the lower 48 states.
在 HN 中查看
coldtea 2026/04/03 00:03
大陆“可能”包括阿拉斯加（甚至在美国地名委员会的官方定义中），但实际上，当“美国大陆”被随意提及时，很少暗示包括在内。大多数人将其与连续的可互换使用。
Continental "could" include Alaska (it's even in the official U.S. Board on Geographic Names definition), but in practice when "continental US" are casually mentioned, it's rarely implied as included. Most use it as interchangeable with contiguous.
在 HN 中查看
cwmoore 2026/04/03 06:47
连续状态是正确的术语。

“大陆”指的是欧洲。

Contiguous states is the correct term.

“Continental” would be in Europe.

在 HN 中查看
coldtea 2026/04/03 00:02
>阿拉斯加与美国毗邻，但需要穿越加拿大部分地区才能通过陆路到达。

连续意味着 48 个连接（连续）状态。它从不包括阿拉斯加。

尽管定义/官方的大陆可能包括它（它位于同一大陆），但通常使用的“美国大陆”也不意味着包括阿拉斯加。

>where-as Alaska is contiguous with the United States, but requires crossing through parts of Canada to reach by land.

Contiguous means the 48 connected (contiguous) states. It never includes Alaska.

And even though definitionally/officially continental could include it (it's in the same continent), in common use "continental US" is not meant to include Alaska either.

在 HN 中查看
umanwizard 2026/04/03 06:43
这就像说美国与日本毗邻，你只需要穿过太平洋的部分地区就可以到达那里。连续意味着你不需要跨越任何其他东西就能到达那里，它是相连的。
That’s like saying the US is contiguous with Japan, you just have to cross through parts of the Pacific Ocean to get there. Contiguous precisely means you don’t have to cross anything else to get there, it is connected.

kjkjadksj 2026/04/02 23:46
我之前曾多次在同一天从海平面上升到 11,000 英尺。我想说的是，海拔效应确实存在，但没有你想象的那么严重。气喘吁吁的时间要快一点，恢复的时间要长一点。不知道在更高的海拔或更大的每日海拔高度增量会是什么样子。
I went from sea level to 11k feet in the same day many times before. I would say the altitude effect is there but not as much as you might expect. A little quicker to be out of breath a little longer to recover it. Not sure what it is like at higher elevations or greater daily altitude delta.
在 HN 中查看
dlenski 2026/04/03 09:08
相同的。我曾多次攀登亚当斯山（海拔 12,280 英尺），其中一次是在海拔 9,500 英尺处过夜，还有一次是在一天内完成整个上升和下降。

爬山很累，下山也很累，但我从来没有感觉到一丝与海拔有关的不适。

我住在海平面附近，日常活动中很少去海拔超过 1,000 英尺的地方。

Same. I've climbed Mount Adams (12,280 feet) several times, including once with an overnight stay at 9,500 feet as well as other times when I did the whole ascent and descent in a single day.

It's a tiring climb and a tiring descent, but I never felt a hint of altitude-related discomfort.

I lived near sea level and didn't often go anywhere more than about 1,000 feet above sea level in daily activities.

在 HN 中查看
prasadjoglekar 2026/04/02 23:50
很大程度上取决于年龄、休息和一般状况。我在 1 天内从海平面爬到了派克峰 14K 的高度，非常不舒服。我做到了，但是住在丹佛的那些身体健康水平比我低的人做得更好。
Very much dependent on age, rest and general conditioning. I went from sea level to 14K at Pikes peak in 1 day and it was quite uncomfortable. I managed, but folks who lived in Denver with lower physical fitness levels than me, did better.
在 HN 中查看
linsomniac 2026/04/03 00:02
同意，我们住在约 5K 的地方，去了派克峰；我和我的妻子没有任何问题（除了轻微的头痛），但我儿子的嘴唇变蓝，他感觉很糟糕。

那次旅行中的其他有趣的事情：我们7月3日去那里，下雪了。 出发前我们在科罗拉多斯普林斯给车充了电，到达山顶时电量还剩36%。 我的妻子担心我们无法回来。 返回 CS 时电量还剩 70%。

Agreed, we live at ~5K and went up to Pikes Peak; my wife and I had no problems (beyond minor headache), but my son's lips were turning blue and he was feeling pretty bad.

Other amusing things from that trip: we went up there the 3rd of July, and it snowed. We charged the car in Colorado Springs before we left, got up to the peak with 36% battery remaining. My wife worried we wouldn't be able to make it back. Got back to CS with ~70% battery left.

在 HN 中查看
nonameiguess 2026/04/03 04:45
我想健身与其他评论所说的有所不同。当我们在后备军官训练队时，我和前妻住在长滩（这显然是海平面），我们经常去圣戈戈尔尼奥进行一日游，并在大约五个小时内步行到山顶，海拔约 11,500 英尺。这一次没有产生任何明显的效果，但我们当时都是非常认真的跑步者，试图成为陆军军官。另一方面，她在春假期间试图登顶阿空加瓜峰，但由于高原反应而未能成功。就我个人而言，我从未到达过比惠特尼山更高的地方。

即使你没有感觉到，海拔高度仍然会产生影响。我记得在高中夏季越野训练结束时，我在大熊队度过了一天两次的地狱般的一周，在那周结束时，那里有 5 千米。我们在海平面上的表现都比典型的要糟糕，有点有趣的是，我记得世界高中边缘的一名高中生（他住在那里）在我参加那场比赛的第一年获得了总成绩第二名，仅仅因为他习惯了海拔，就击败了经验丰富的竞争对手。

它也可以反向工作。在我的科罗拉多州装甲基础军官课程中，有一名军官在我们处理的第一周的两英里测试中给自己带来了横纹肌颤动，显然是因为他太习惯了海拔，还没有完全适应诺克斯堡的气氛。

I guess fitness makes a difference from what these other comments are saying. My ex-wife and I lived in Long Beach (which is obviously sea level) when we were in ROTC and pretty regularly took day trips out to San Gogornio and walked to the summit in about five hours, which is ~11,500ft. Not once did that have any noticeable effect, but we were both pretty serious runners back in the day trying to become Army officers. On the other hand, she tried to summit Aconcagua during a spring break and couldn't make it due to altitude sickness. I've never been higher than Mt. Whitney, personally.

Even if you don't feel it, the altitude still makes a difference, though. I recall doing two-a-day hell weeks at Big Bear at the end of summer cross-country training in high school and there was a 5k up there at the end of that week. We all got worse times than typical at sea level, and somewhat amusingly, I recall a high school senior from Rim of the World High School (who lived up there) getting 2nd place overall the first year I ever competed in that race, beating way more seasoned competitors just because he was used to the altitude.

It works in reverse, too. There was an officer in my Armor Basic Officer Course from Colorado who gave himself rhabdo during the two-mile test the first week we in-processed, apparently because he was so used to altitude that he hadn't quite acclimated to Fort Knox atmosphere.

在 HN 中查看
jltsiren 2026/04/03 06:36
如果说有什么不同的话，那就是健身会让你更容易患高原反应。这不是一种固有的影响，而是你的习惯驱使你做你不应该做的事情。适应高海拔时，你应该采取懒惰和缓慢的态度。但如果你身体健康，你可能会习惯动作太快、逼自己太紧。你可能不会像你预期的那样很快地从劳累中恢复过来，而且你可能每天都会爬得比你应该爬的更高。

高原反应通常在 12 至 24 小时后开始。如果您在同一天爬到高处并返回，通常没有足够的时间来出现症状。 11,500 英尺并不是那么高的海拔。人们经常从海平面飞往库斯科、拉巴斯、拉萨和列城，其中大多数人没有受到严重影响。

If anything, fitness makes you more susceptible to altitude sickness. It's not an inherent effect, but rather your habits driving you to do things you shouldn't. You are supposed to take things lazy and slow when acclimatizing to high altitude. But if you're fit, you may be used to moving too fast and pushing yourself too hard. You may not recover from exertion as quickly as you expect, and you may end up climbing higher every day than you should.

Altitude sickness typically starts after 12–24 hours. If you climb high and come back down in the same day, there is usually not enough time for the symptoms to start. And 11,500ft is not that high altitude. People routinely fly to Cuzco, La Paz, Lhasa, and Leh from sea level, and most of them suffer no serious ill effects.

在 HN 中查看
simplyluke 2026/04/03 07:01
在攀岩界，人们普遍认为海拔的短期主要影响是心血管健康状况的降低。

您的心脏越能更好地将氧气输送到肌肉和器官中，就越能更好地补偿氧气不足。

这不是解决高原反应的万全之策，但它绝对是众多重要变量之一。确实，有些人更容易受到影响，无论如何，我有一些参加马拉松比赛的朋友，他们从海平面飞往丹佛时感到头痛欲裂。

It's pretty widely accepted in the climbing world that the primary effect of altitude in the short-term is a reduction in your cardiovascular fitness.

The better your heart is at getting oxygen into your muscles and organs, the better it can compensate for less oxygen.

Not a bulletproof solution to altitude sickness, but it's definitely one of a lot of variables that matters. It's also just true that some people are way more susceptible regardless, I've got friends who run competitive marathon times who get splitting headaches flying from sea level to denver.

在 HN 中查看
hermitcrab 2026/04/03 05:58
>我认为健身会有所作为

并不真地。高原反应似乎对谁影响最严重是相当随机的。许多年前，我徒步登上了梅拉峰（~21,000 英尺）的山顶。我们一行人中有3名身体最健康的人出现了高原反应，未能登顶。

>I guess fitness makes a difference

Not really. Altitude sickness seems quite random in who it effects worst. I trekked to the top of Mera Peak (~21,000 ft) many years ago. 3 of the fittest people in our party got altitude sickness and didn't make it to the peak.

在 HN 中查看
quickthrowman 2026/04/03 03:15
几个月前，我乘坐空中电车在不到一个小时的时间内从海拔约 500 英尺（棕榈泉）上升到海拔 8,500 英尺（圣哈辛托峰），这一点非常明显，我的步行速度下降了三分之一，而且我的呼吸比平时更加​​困难。
I went from ~500 ft above sea level (Palm Springs) up to 8,500 feet above sea level (San Jacinto Peak) in less than an hour via the aeria tram a couple months ago and it was very noticeable, my walking speed fell by a third and I was breathing a lot harder than I usually do.

chimeracoder 2026/04/03 00:54
> 12-15K 英尺在计划中并不算高。美国西部的许多山峰都在这个范围内或更高。

它“没有那么高”，但人们经常因为这些态度甚至更低的态度而患上 AMS。

> 12-15K feet just isn’t that high in the scheme of things. Many peaks in the western US are in that range or more.

It's "not that high", but people frequently do get AMS at those attitudes or even lower.

在 HN 中查看
tim333 2026/04/03 04:57
在通常情况下没问题的海拔高度进行一日游与在那么高的高度睡觉（如果不适应就会导致问题）之间存在很大差异。
There's a big difference between doing a day trip to those altitudes which is normally ok, and sleeping that high which causes problems if not acclimatised.
在 HN 中查看
chimeracoder 2026/04/03 05:12
> 在通常情况下没问题的海拔高度进行一日游与在那么高的地方睡觉（如果不适应就会导致问题）之间存在很大差异。

我不确定你想表达什么观点。人们经常在远低于 OP 提到的高度时经历 AMS，无论是在到达当天还是在第 2-4 天，这甚至没有考虑到剧烈的体力活动。

> There's a big difference between doing a day trip to those altitudes which is normally ok, and sleeping that high which causes problems if not acclimatised.

I'm not sure what point you're trying to make. People regularly experience AMS at the heights far below what OP mentioned, whether on the day they arrive or on days 2-4, and that's not even accounting for strenuous physical activity.

在 HN 中查看
delichon 2026/04/02 22:10
> 但是导游和酒店工作人员……告诉他们，他们有死亡的危险，只有立即疏散才能拯救他们。

我在海拔 11,000 英尺的高度患上了急性高山症。头痛、恶心、头晕、疲劳。我昏过去了，直到摔在地上才醒过来。我非常迷失方向并且很脆弱。如果有人告诉我必须去医院，否则我就会死，他们可能会像驯服的山羊一样牵着我。他们可能是对的。如果您患有高原脑水肿或肺水肿，则会危及生命。

获得回扣的导游只需缩短无聊的适应时间，就可以提高获得回扣的可能性。

> But guides and hotel staff ... tell them they are at risk of dying, that only immediate evacuation will save them.

I got Acute Mountain Sickness at just 11k feet. Headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue. I passed out until hitting the ground woke me up. I was very disoriented and vulnerable. If someone had told me that I had to get to a hospital or I'd die they could have led me like a tame goat. And they could be right. If you have high-altitude cerebral or pulmonary edema it is life threatening.

A guide getting a kickback can make it a lot more likely just by cutting short the boring acclimatization time.

在 HN 中查看
meroes 2026/04/02 22:45
是的，这对你来说是一个非常严重的情况。我知道当我父亲几乎无法站立但坚持让我们徒步 1000 英尺回来然后再下来时，情况也很严重。但当我们回到家时，我了解到高原反应是多么致命。
Ya that was a very serious situation for you. I knew when my dad was barely able to stand but insisted we hike the 1000ft back up to then get back down it was also serious. But when we got home I read how deadly altitude sickness is.

skilled 2026/04/02 21:18
我在2015年底进行了珠穆朗玛峰大本营徒步旅行，当时很常见（我自己亲眼所见，也听说过），人们会徒步上去，但下山时他们会假装腿部/背部受伤或归咎于高原反应，只要你有合适的保险，加德满都的直升机就会来接你。
I did the Everest base camp trek in late 2015, at that time it was quite common (saw it myself and heard about it) that people would do the trek up but to get down they would fake a leg/back injury or blame altitude sickness and the chopper from Kathmandu would come pick you up, as long as you had the right insurance.

WarmWash 2026/04/02 22:33
当然，保险公司很快就会意识到这一点，我的意思是，有些人的工作是监控所有索赔并相应地调整价格。

因此，虽然保险公司可能感觉被骗了，但几乎可以肯定的是，被保险人没有乘坐直升机。

Surely the insurers would quickly become aware of this, I mean, there are people whose job it is to monitor all claims and adjust prices accordingly.

So while it might feel like the insurers were getting fleeced, it was almost certainly the insured who didn't get the copter ride.

stronglikedan 2026/04/03 05:15

bell-cot 2026/04/02 23:18
不是保险承保人 - 但当您在海外爬山、在北极或南极 X 英里范围内进行洞穴探险、乘坐潜艇旅行或以其他方式冒险进入“高额开采”领域时，明显的反措施难道不是排除医疗援助/交通保险吗？
Not an insurance underwriter - but wouldn't the obvious counter-move be to exclude coverage for medical assistance/transportation when you're climbing mountains overseas, spelunking, within X miles of the north or south pole, traveling in a submarine, or have otherwise ventured into "high-dollar extraction" territory?
在 HN 中查看
Ectiseethe 2026/04/02 23:39
两年前我去了尼泊尔。我的万事达金卡的标准保险特别将急性高原反应的医疗援助/交通排除在承保范围之外（并且救援人员在没有适当保险承保证明的情况下不愿意进行干预）。

作为预防措施（在论坛上阅读过相关内容），我从一家专门从事徒步旅行和登山的法国商店（le Vieux Campeur）购买了额外的保险，以承保更多活动。

我做了一件好事，因为我最终不得不因最初被认为是急性高原反应的疾病而被疏散，但事实证明，一旦进入医院，我的生命就会受到更大的威胁。

I went to Nepal two years ago. The standard insurance of my Mastercard Gold specifically excluded medical assistance/transportation for acute altitude sickness from the coverage (and rescue operators are reluctant to intervene without proof of proper insurance coverage).

As a precaution (having read about it on forums) I had taken an additional insurance from a French shop specialized in hiking and mountaineering (le Vieux Campeur) to cover more events.

Good thing I did because I ended up having to be evacuated for something that was initially considered as acute altitude sickness and turned out to be a lot more life threatening once in the hospital.

在 HN 中查看
fwipsy 2026/04/02 23:25
明显的反击举措就是收取更高的保费。无论危机是真实的还是捏造的，它都有效。真正的输家不是保险公司，而是其他多付保费的游客。
The obvious counter move is just to charge higher premiums. It works whether the crises are real or fabricated. The real losers are not the insurance companies, but other tourists overpaying on their premiums.

在 HN 中查看
jltsiren 2026/04/03 02:24
根据我的经验，只有与信用卡和各种会员资格捆绑在一起的廉价保险单才会将高海拔徒步旅行视为高风险活动。您作为个人购买的任何旅行保险都应涵盖该保险。如果有海拔限制，通常是6000米，所以珠穆朗玛峰大本营和乞力马扎罗山会被覆盖，但阿空加瓜不会。任何实际的登山活动都具有高风险，无论海拔高度如何。
In my experience, it's only the cheap insurance policies bundled with credit cards and various memberships that consider high-altitude hiking a high-risk activity. Any travel insurance you buy as individual should cover it. If there is an altitude limit, it's usually 6000 m, so Everest Base Camp and Kilimanjaro would be covered but Aconcagua wouldn't. And any actual mountaineering would also be high-risk, regardless of altitude.

Normal travel insurance doesn't cover high-risk activities like mountaineering. You have to buy a specialist policy.

MikeNotThePope 2026/04/02 21:30
我可以准确地告诉你这对我来说意味着什么。 我从 EBC 徒步旅行中海拔最高/最后的城镇戈拉克谢普 (Gorakshep) 乘坐直升机前往卢卡拉 (Lukla)，这个疯狂的机场被人们称为世界上最危险的机场。 对我来说，2024 年 10 月，一个体重 255 磅/115 公斤的家伙、两个身高只有我一半的尼泊尔人、一名飞行员以及我们不太重的徒步装备，价格为 2000 美元。

图片/视频： https://www.instagram.com/p/DBTpLGtydZW/

I can tell you exactly what it cost for me. I took the helicopter from Gorakshep, the highest/last town on the EBC trek, to Lukla, the crazy airport people call the most dangerous one in the world. For me, a 255 lbs / 115kg guy, 2 Nepalis that are each half my size, a pilot, and our not-that-heavy hiking gear was 2000 USD in October of 2024.

Pics/video: https://www.instagram.com/p/DBTpLGtydZW/

datadrivenangel 2026/04/02 21:30
尼泊尔是一个低收入和高度腐败的国家，政府和正规商业结构非常不稳定，即使对于政府调查人员来说，“小费”也变得很常见......

这基本上是每个人获得更多游客收入的一种方式，这是尼泊尔的主要出口产品之一。

Nepal is a low income and high corruption country, where the government and formal business structures are unstable enough that 'tipping' becomes common even for government investigators...

It's basically a way for everyone to get more tourists dollars, which is one of Nepals primary exports.

umanwizard 2026/04/03 06:49

在 HN 中查看
bombcar 2026/04/02 21:19
如果单个保险公司的成本足够低（几百万），并且他们并没有真正面临突然爆炸的风险，并且他们减轻的成本也是数百万（或者有杀死客户的风险），那么他们就不太可能改善。搏击俱乐部，但相反。

然而，如果他们联合起来，他们可能会做一些事情 - 但这可能会导致其他问题（当地保险公司成为 仅有的 提供保险等）。

If the cost to an individual insurance company is low enough (in the few millions) and they're not really at risk of it suddenly exploding, and the cost for them to mitigate is also in the millions (or risks killing a customer), they're unlikely to improve. Fight Club, but the other way around.

However, if they all gang up together they might do something - but that can cause other issues (a local insurer becomes the only insurance available, etc).

psadri 2026/04/02 22:59

在 HN 中查看
rdtsc 2026/04/02 21:45
> 但这些都不起作用“由于惩罚措施不严，骗局仍在继续”

它渗透了上去。这通常是腐败所发生的情况。如果发现下级有一个有利可图的计划，上级（审计师、警察、立法者）就会大惊小怪地公开阻挠，但在幕后却会要求削减。

> But none of that worked “The scam continued due to lax punitive action,”

It percolated up. It’s usually what happens with corruption. If lower levels are found out to have a lucrative scheme, the higher ups (auditors, police, legislators) make a big fuss about stumping it publicly, but behind the scenes go and ask for a cut.

pRusya 2026/04/02 23:30
一个比尼泊尔更古老的故事（误导游客）。 6 个月前的一篇文章通过 HN 讨论中的更多例子展示了政府如何对待自己的人民。 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45166972

较少讨论的是那些能够识别骗局并拒绝让它发生的人的遭遇。

A story older than Nepal (misleading tourists). And an article from 6 months ago shows how the govt treats its own people with more examples in HN discussion. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45166972

What is less discussed is what happened to people who were able to identify the scam and refused to let it happen.

kaudinya 2026/04/03 05:35
从那时起，政府就被 Z 世代的抗议推翻了。临时政府领导了全国选举，由年轻领导人领导的年轻政党赢得了议会2/3多数席位。

新议会在很多方面都发生了根本性的转变。如果你碰巧读到了这个进展，你会觉得它非常好莱坞式。

Since then, the govt has been overthrown by a Gen Z protest. An interim govt led the national elections and a young party led by young leaders have won 2/3 majority in the parliament.

The new parliament is a radical departure in many ways. Its very hollywoodish if you happen to read the development.

在 HN 中查看
pkoiralap 2026/04/03 06:26
虽然导游和企业主确实总是在寻找赚取额外现金的机会，但报道却有点偏差。

AMS 类似症状的开始很容易被误认为是行走疲劳和脱水。如果您处于休息状态，则更容易识别，但在长途跋涉期间，这种情况很少见。因此，当您真正开始意识到出现问题时，您的风险就已经很高了。在这种情况下，唯一有效的办法就是尽可能快地下降。

考虑到如果你玩弄 AMS 绝对会 100% 杀死你，导游向徒步旅行者提供直升机救援的选择并没有那么糟糕，至少如果你考虑到可能发生的最坏情况的话。

While it is true that guides and business owners are always looking for opportunities to earn extra cash, the reporting is a tiny bit off here.

Start of AMS like symptoms can easily be mistaken for walking fatigue and dehydration. It is easier to identify if you are at rest, but during the trek that is seldom the case. So when you actually start realizing something is wrong, you already are at an elevated risk. The only thing that works in these cases is to descend and as fast as possible at that.

Considering the fact that AMS will absolutely and a 100% kill you if you play around with it, guides presenting trekkers with an option of helicopter rescue is not that bad, at least if you look at the worst that can happen.

givemeethekeys 2026/04/02 21:26

Sounds pretty bad until you look at the numbers.

RajT88 2026/04/02 22:25
然后看起来很糟糕？

每起事件的数量相当低，并且可能对当地社区的资助有很大帮助。

但事故的数量却是惊人的——每年远远超过 1000 起。

And then it looks very bad?

The amount of each incident is fairly low, and probably goes a long way to funding the local community.

But the number of incidents is nuts - well over 1000 per year.

datadrivenangel 2026/04/02 21:27

Rescue insurance was required by the trekking company when I did EBC a few years back.
在 HN 中查看
tristor 2026/04/02 23:49
作为一个在科罗拉多州完成过不少 14 人制比赛的人，其中许多我的状态都不是很好，大多数人都没有获得 AMS，尤其是低于 12k 的人，因此本文中的数字肯定看起来像是有人在伪造或被欺骗。 大多数时候，如果您的海拔高度超过 10k，您只需要更好地补充水分就可以了。
As someone who has done quite a few 14ers in Colorado, many of which I wasn't in the greatest shape for, most people do not get AMS especially below 12k, and therefore the numbers in this article definitely do look like somebody somewhere is faking it or being defrauded. Much of the time you just need to hydrate better if you're going up in elevation above 10k and you'll be fine.
在 HN 中查看
justonceokay 2026/04/02 23:56
专业提示：如果您在 13000 英尺的高空向您的妻子求婚，实际上不要让她戴上戒指。她的手指可能会肿胀，导致她对血液循环感到恐慌，你将不得不跑下山，直到今天，她不会认为这像你那么有趣
Pro-tip: if you propose to your wife at 13000 feet, don’t actually have her put the ring on. Her fingers may swell causing her to panic about circulation and you’ll have to run down the mountain and to this day she will not think it is as funny as you
在 HN 中查看
tristor 2026/04/03 04:19
不过，这也是一个值得将来与新朋友分享的好故事。 我也有一张，呵呵 日落时分，我在当地湖上一艘帆船的船头向我现在的妻子求婚，俯瞰着水面。 本来应该是一个令人惊奇的时刻，只是直到那次旅行我才知道我现在的妻子晕船/晕动病。 幸运的是，这并没有毁掉一切，但从那以后，除了塔林和赫尔辛基之间的一次渡轮之外，我们再也没有一起坐过船。
It makes a good story to share with new friends in the future though. I've got one of those too, heh. I proposed to my now-wife on the bow of a sailboat at sunset on a local lake, overlooking the water. Should have been an amazing moment, except I didn't know until that trip that my now-wife gets seasick/motion sickness. Luckily it did not ruin things, but we've never been on a boat together since except for a ferry crossing between Tallinn and Helsinki once.

在 HN 中查看
adolph 2026/04/03 02:57
令我惊讶的是，任何登顶者都能获得保险。我认为，如果欺诈率低至 3.5%，并且专门为这次旅行签订了保险，那么理性的付款人会提高费率并继续下去。

总体而言，某些资产（例如直升机）的容量是有限的。如果紧急情况承载能力为 X，真正的紧急情况为 0.6 X，则损坏容量为 0.4 X，其中放置了欺诈性紧急情况，使系统中的每个人保持完整，以便当真正的紧急情况接近 0.9 X 时，无需欺诈。这遵循“最佳欺诈量非零”，消除这种欺诈可能会消除系统存在所需的余量。

  An anecdote tells of the British government's bounty on dead Indian cobras 
  giving locals the perverse incentive to start breeding the snakes, to be able 
  to kill more of them and collect more bounty
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perverse_incentive
It is a surprise to me that anyone summiting has access to insurance at all. I suppose that if the fraud rate is as low as 3.5% and insurance is contracted specifically for the trip, then a rational payor will raise rates and carry on.

On the whole, there is finite capacity of certain assets, like helicopters. If the emergency carrying capacity is X and true emergencies are .6 X then there is spoiled capacity of .4 X, in which fraudulent emergencies are placed, keeping everyone in the system whole so that when true emergencies approach .9 X there is no need for fraud. This follows the "optimal amount of fraud is non-zero" and eliminating this fraud might remove the margin needed for the system to exist at all.

  An anecdote tells of the British government's bounty on dead Indian cobras 
  giving locals the perverse incentive to start breeding the snakes, to be able 
  to kill more of them and collect more bounty
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perverse_incentive

tim333 2026/04/03 05:11
文章中的人只是在山谷中跋涉，而不是登顶。不久前我尝试了一次登顶，并没有费心购买保险，因为他们能做的不多——直升机不能飞那么高，用现金找一些登山者带你下来会更好。
The people in the article are just trekking around the valleys, not going for the summit. I did a summit attempt a while back and didn't bother with insurance because here wasn't much they could do - helicopters didn't go that high and getting some sherpers to carry you down would be better achieved with cash.

IAmBroom 2026/04/02 21:02
“在调查中至少提到的一个案例中，发酵粉被混入食物中，导致游客身体不适。”

我发现过度食用的唯一不良影响是“舌头上有刺痛感”。当然，这并不意味着“投毒者”对此一无所知，并且真心实意地想让他们生病。或者也许他们只是说，“如果你感到舌头刺痛，你就快死了！！！”。

"In at least one case cited in the investigation, baking powder was mixed into food to make tourists physically unwell."

The only ill effect I can find from overconsumption is a "tingly sensation on the tongue". Of course, that doesn't mean the 'poisoner' wasn't ignorant of this, and genuinely did it trying to make them sick. Or maybe they simply said, "If you feel your tongue tingling, YOU ARE DYING!!!".

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/02 21:18
当你把它弄湿时，它是一种发酵剂，所以吞咽足够多肯定会感觉消化不良，因为所有的气体。
It’s a leavener when you get it wet, so swallowing enough will definitely feel like digestive upset from all the gas.
在 HN 中查看
ambicapter 2026/04/02 21:15
我觉得这里的“使身体不适”只是意味着他们会尝到一些恶心的东西，却没有意识到这是发酵粉，并将这种感觉视为身体出了问题。我知道混合小苏打和泡打粉会给我带来一些味道非常难闻的食物。
I feel like "make physically unwell" here just means they'll taste something gross, not realize it is the baking powder, and treat the feeling as if something is wrong with their body. I know mixing up baking soda and baking powder has made for some pretty unpleasant tasting food for me.

datadrivenangel 2026/04/02 21:32
是的，这就是为什么很容易说服人们撤离。 确实有人在珠穆朗玛峰上死亡，包括仅因高原反应而进行的 EBC 徒步旅行，因此严重的症状通常会导致徒步下山。
Yes, which is why it's easy to then convince people to evacuate. People do die on Everest, including EBC treks from altitude sickness alone, so severe symptoms usually lead to taking the trekking back down the mountain.

tokai 2026/04/02 23:37
一些运动员使用发酵粉，因为它被证明可以提高成绩。[0]我发现他们添加它是为了达到这种效果，这比让任何人感到不适更现实。因为它并不是真正让你生病的东西。

yard2010 2026/04/02 21:21
问题不在于这个具体案例（那些保险公司不会破产），而在于系统。当你没有适当的管理时，你就无法像适当的管理那样有效地进行合作。这是症状，而不是问题。

想象一下减少合作的代价——当采取极端措施时，保险公司不会接受为在那里徒步旅行的人提供保险。价格会上涨。这将伤害行业和徒步旅行者。

正确的管理>利润

The problem is not in this specific case (those insurance companies won't go bankrupt), but with the system. When you don't have a proper administration you can't really cooperate as effectively as with proper administration. This is the symptom, not the problem.

Imaging the price of less cooperation - when taken to the extreme the insurance company won't accept to insure people trekking there. The price will go up. This will hurt both the industry and the trekkers.

Proper administration > profit

miltonlost 2026/04/02 21:36
停止无意义的爬山和破坏自然环境。此时攀登珠穆朗玛峰只是炫耀性消费的标志，除了金钱之外并没有任何成就。最好把钱花在火上。
Stop pointlessly climbing mountains and ruining the natural environment. Climbing Mt Everest at this point is just a sign of conspicuous consumption and not any achievement other than financial. Would have been better to spend your money lighting it on fire.
在 HN 中查看
datadrivenangel 2026/04/02 21:47
这主要是徒步相关的疏散，比较容易，影响也较小。每人 EBC 总体成本比登顶尝试便宜 100 倍，甚至 500 倍。

萨加玛塔国家公园和整个山谷直至 EBC 是世界上令人惊叹的美丽部分。

This is mostly trekking related evacuation, which is far easy and lower impact. EBC is about 100x cheaper overall per person than summit attempts, if not 500x.

And Sagarmartha national park and the whole valley up to EBC is an amazingly beautiful part of the world.

simojo 2026/04/02 22:27
公平地说，这条道路通常在一年中的大部分时间都被积雪覆盖，因此人流的影响很小。然而，大部分保护措施都是固定的，如果有什么东西被撕掉，可能会产生持久的影响。

对于其他夏季山顶干燥的山脉，我同意：侵蚀的影响是可怕的

to be fair, the approach is usually covered in snowpack for most of the year, so impact is minimal by foot traffic. However, most of the protection is fixed, which could have lasting effects if something were to rip out.

For other mountains with dry summits in the summers, I would agree: the effects of erosion are frightening
